title: Deep Learning Essentials
pitchHtml: Deep learning is the engine behind the AI that makes headlines — image recognition, speech, translation, and ultimately the large language models in later modules. But for a tech lead, the headline is also the trap: deep learning is more expensive, more data-hungry, and harder to staff than the classical ML of Classical ML & Forecasting in Practice, and it only earns that cost on a specific kind of problem. This module gives you the intuition — what a neural network actually is, how it learns, why it needs GPUs — without a single derivation. The one idea to walk away with, the one that reshapes every cost estimate, is transfer learning: you almost never train a deep model from scratch; you start from a pretrained one and adapt it. That single move collapses the data, compute, and talent bill from "research lab" to "a small team in a few weeks" — and it's the difference between a deep-learning mission you can sell and one you can't.

items.html: Decide when deep learning beats classical ML — and, just as often, when it doesn't — from the data type and scale
Explain a neural network to a stakeholder as stacked transformations that learn representations, with no equations
Describe training intuitively (loss, gradient descent, backprop as "blame assignment") and name the knobs — epochs, batch size, learning rate
Estimate the compute/GPU cost of a deep-learning effort and factor it into a quote
Explain transfer learning & fine-tuning and why they collapse the cost and data needed for a deep-learning mission
Recognise embeddings as the reusable by-product of deep models (forward-link to NLP & Embeddings in Practice)
Scope a transfer-learning classifier, a feasibility study, or a "add a pretrained capability" mission with a named deliverable and buyer

subsections.title: When Deep Learning Beats Classical ML — and When It Doesn't

blocks.html: This is the decision that matters most, and it is mostly settled by the shape of your data, not by ambition. Deep learning's superpower is learning useful features automatically from raw, unstructured inputs — the pixels of an image, the waveform of audio, the raw characters of text — where hand-engineering features is hopeless. On structured / tabular data, that superpower is wasted: the features are already columns, and gradient-boosted trees beat deep nets on cost, speed, and usually accuracy too (the core lesson of Classical ML & Forecasting in Practice).

Your data is…Default toWhy
Tabular (rows & columns, mixed types)Gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost)Cheaper, faster, explainable, usually more accurate — no GPU
Images / videoDeep learning (CNNs / vision transformers)Raw pixels need learned features; classical ML can't see
Audio / speechDeep learningWaveforms/spectrograms are unstructured; covered in Computer Vision & Multimodal in Practice
Raw text / languageDeep learning (transformers, embeddings)Meaning lives in sequence & context — see NLP & Embeddings in Practice
Small data (hundreds–low thousands of rows)Classical ML (or transfer learning, never from-scratch DL)Deep nets overfit badly without lots of data
Massive data + complex patternsDeep learningDL keeps improving with scale where classical ML plateaus

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Two forces decide it: data type and data scale. Unstructured data (you can't write the features by hand) pushes toward deep learning; large scale with rich patterns lets deep learning keep climbing while classical methods flatten out. If your data is tabular and modest in size — which describes the majority of business problems — classical ML is not the cautious choice, it's usually the better one. Reach for deep learning when the input is something a human perceives rather than tabulates.

The senior framing for a stakeholder who asks "shouldn't we use deep learning, it's more powerful?": power you don't need is just cost. Deep learning is essential for perception and language and overkill for a spreadsheet. Most roadmaps have some of each — route each problem by its data, not by what sounds advanced.

subsections.title: What a Neural Network Actually Is

blocks.html:
Strip away the mystique and a neural network is a stack of simple transformations, each one reshaping the data a little, learned end-to-end so the final output is useful.

  • Layers as stacked transformations. The input (pixels, numbers, a text encoding) passes through a sequence of layers. Each layer takes the previous layer's output, multiplies it by a set of weights, adds a bias, and passes it through a simple activation function. Stack enough of these and the network can represent very intricate input→output relationships.
  • Weights are what's learned. A weight is a dial. A network has millions or billions of them. "Training" means turning all those dials until the output is right. The weights are the model — everything the network "knows" is encoded in their values.
  • Activation functions add the bends. Without them, stacking layers would just be one big linear transformation — a straight line, no matter how many layers (this is why Classical ML & Forecasting in Practice noted a straight line can't fit a wiggly boundary). The activation (today usually ReLU — "keep positives, zero out negatives") lets each layer bend the space, so the stack can fit curves, corners, and interactions.
  • "Learning representations" — the key phrase. This is the real magic and the reason deep learning wins on unstructured data. Early layers learn crude features (edges in an image, character patterns in text); deeper layers combine them into richer concepts (shapes → objects → "a cat"). The network invents its own features instead of you hand-crafting them — which is exactly the labour classical ML asks of you in feature engineering (Working with Data: Sources, Quality & Feature Engineering).

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Picture an assembly line. Raw material (pixels) enters; each station (layer) applies one simple operation and passes the result on; by the end, a recognisable product (a label, a translation) comes out. Nobody programmed "what a cat looks like" — the line learned the right operations from millions of examples. "Deep" just means the line has many stations; depth is what lets it build complex concepts from simple parts. That's the whole idea — no equations required.
The only formula worth seeing, just to anchor "weights and a bend," is what one layer does:
output=activation(W⋅input+b)\text{output} = \text{activation}(W \cdot \text{input} + b)output=activation(W⋅input+b)

That's it. A network is this, repeated, with the WWW's and bbb's learned. Everything else is engineering on top of this one move.

The activation functions you'll hear named. The activation is the "bend" — and a handful are standard. You won't choose them, but you should recognise each, what it's for, and its catch:

ActivationFormulaUse caseWhy / catch
Sigmoidσ(x)=11+e−x\sigma(x)=\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}σ(x)=1+e−x1​Binary output layer — squashes any number to a probability in (0,1)(0,1)(0,1)Saturates at the ends (slope → 0), so gradients vanish in deep stacks — avoid in hidden layers
Tanhex−e−xex+e−x\dfrac{e^{x}-e^{-x}}{e^{x}+e^{-x}}ex+e−xex−e−x​Zero-centred alternative to sigmoid, range (−1,1)(-1,1)(−1,1)Outputs centred on 0 ease optimisation; still saturates at the extremes
ReLUmax⁡(0,x)\max(0,x)max(0,x)Default for hidden layersCheap (a threshold), doesn't saturate for positives → mitigates vanishing gradients. Catch: a neuron stuck at negative inputs outputs 0 forever — the "dying ReLU"
Leaky ReLUmax⁡(αx,x)\max(\alpha x, x)max(αx,x), small α\alphaα (e.g. 0.01)Drop-in for ReLU when neurons dieThe small negative slope keeps a trickle of gradient alive, so neurons can recover — fixes dying ReLU
Softmaxezi∑jezj\dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}∑j​ezj​ezi​​Multi-class output layerTurns a vector of scores into a probability distribution — all outputs positive and summing to 1

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Sigmoid and tanh "squash" — gentle in the middle, flat at the ends; that flatness is exactly what kills gradients deep in a network, which is why ReLU ("keep positives, zero the rest") became the hidden-layer default — it stays responsive and costs almost nothing. The output layer is chosen by the task: sigmoid for one yes/no probability, softmax for "pick one of many" — it exponentiates each score (making them positive) then normalises so they sum to 1, giving a clean probability distribution over the classes.

Training a neural network is the same loop you met for any model in Math & Stats Intuition for Leaders, scaled up:

  1. Loss — a single number measuring how wrong the network's predictions are on the training data. Lower is better. Training = making the loss small. The choice of loss follows the task. For classification it's cross-entropy:
L=−∑kyklog⁡(y^k)L = -\sum_k y_k \log(\hat{y}_k)L=−k∑​yk​log(y^​k​)

where yky_kyk​ is the true label (1 for the correct class, 0 otherwise) and y^k\hat{y}_ky^​k​ is the model's predicted probability for class kkk — so the loss is simply −log⁡(probability the model gave the right answer)-\log(\text{probability the model gave the right answer})−log(probability the model gave the right answer), which blows up when the model is confidently wrong. For regression (predicting a number) it's mean squared error, the average of (y^i−yi)2(\hat{y}_i - y_i)^2(y^​i​−yi​)2 over the examples — the same MSE you met in Classical ML & Forecasting in Practice.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Cross-entropy pairs naturally with softmax / sigmoid outputs (above): those produce probabilities, and cross-entropy is the right yardstick for "how good is a predicted probability." Together they punish confident mistakes hard and produce a clean, well-behaved gradient — which is exactly why this pairing is the default for every classifier. MSE is the analogue for continuous targets: penalise the squared distance from the truth.

1. Gradient descent — the optimisation recap from Math & Stats Intuition for Leaders: from where you are, find the downhill direction in "loss space" and take a small step. Repeat. The loss slides toward a minimum, the weights toward good values. The update rule every practitioner recognises is:
θ←θ−η ∇L(θ)\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta\,\nabla L(\theta)θ←θ−η∇L(θ)

where θ\thetaθ is the full set of weights, ∇L(θ)\nabla L(\theta)∇L(θ) is the gradient (the uphill direction of the loss — so we step the opposite way), and η\etaη (eta) is the learning rate, the size of the step. In practice you don't compute the gradient on all the data at once: SGD / mini-batch estimates it from one small batch of examples at a time — far cheaper and, helpfully, the noise helps escape bad spots. The learning rate is the knob that bites: too large and the steps overshoot the minimum and the loss diverges or oscillates; too small and training crawls and may stall. (Deeper math → Neural Network Foundations in the AI Scientist path.)

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: The gradient is the slope under your feet; the minus sign says "walk downhill"; η\etaη is your stride. Long strides cross the valley faster but can leap clean over the bottom and bounce up the far wall; tiny strides never miss but take forever. Tuning η\etaη is finding the stride that descends fastest without overshooting — which is why it's the single most fiddly knob below.

1. Backpropagation — the part everyone names and few explain. With millions of weights, you need to know how much each one contributed to the error so you know which dials to turn. Backprop is an efficient algorithm that does exactly this blame assignment: it pushes the error backward through the layers and computes each weight's share of the blame, so gradient descent can adjust every dial in one pass. It is the chain rule applied layer by layer — for a single weight the gradient has the shape

blocks.tex: \frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a}\cdot\frac{\partial a}{\partial z}\cdot\frac{\partial z}{\partial w}
blocks.html: ∂L∂w=∂L∂a⋅∂a∂z⋅∂z∂w\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a}\cdot\frac{\partial a}{\partial z}\cdot\frac{\partial z}{\partial w}∂w∂L​=∂a∂L​⋅∂z∂a​⋅∂w∂z​

read right-to-left: how the weight www moves the layer's pre-activation zzz, how zzz moves the activation aaa, how aaa moves the loss LLL — multiplied together to give the weight's total influence on the error. You do not need the full derivation — it's this chain rule applied carefully across every layer, and it's automatic in every framework. The leader's takeaway: backprop is "who's responsible for this mistake, and by how much," computed cheaply for every weight at once.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Blame flows backward like a chain of responsibility: the loss blames the output, the output blames the activation, the activation blames the weight — each link a simple "how much does this nudge that?" multiplied down the chain. Because the links are multiplied, if several are tiny the product vanishes — that's the "vanishing gradients" you'll hear about, and the reason ReLU (§2) replaced the saturating sigmoid in hidden layers. (Full derivation → Neural Network Foundations in the AI Scientist path.)

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Imagine a relay race where the team finishes badly. Backprop walks back down the line asking each runner "how much of the lost time was yours?" — then each adjusts for next time. Gradient descent is running the race again and again, everyone improving their share, until the team is fast. You will hear engineers say "the gradients vanished" or "the loss won't go down" — that's this loop failing, and it's their problem to fix, not yours to derive.
The knobs a leader actually hears about. You don't tune these, but you'll sit in reviews where they're discussed, and you should know what they trade off:

KnobPlain meaningWhat it trades off
EpochOne full pass over all the training dataMore epochs = more learning, but eventually overfitting (§4) and more compute cost
Batch sizeHow many examples the network looks at before updating weightsBigger = faster on a GPU but needs more memory; affects stability
Learning rateHow big a step gradient descent takesToo high → training diverges/explodes; too low → trains forever. The single most fiddly knob

If a team says "we just need more epochs" to fix a quality problem, be sceptical — past a point that's how you memorise the training data, not how you improve.

Why it needs GPUs, and what that costs. All of the above is enormous amounts of matrix multiplication, repeated millions of times. GPUs (and TPUs) do matrix maths massively in parallel — they are to deep learning what an espresso machine is to a café: you can do it by hand (CPU), but not at any useful scale. The cost consequence is real and is the reason deep learning is in a different budget tier from classical ML:

  • A boosted-tree model trains in minutes on a laptop CPU for ~€0. A deep model often needs GPU time billed by the hour (cloud GPUs run from a couple of euros to tens of euros per hour in 2026, depending on the chip).
  • The cost lives in training, not in using the model. Once trained, running ("inference") is comparatively cheap — but training a large model from scratch can mean thousands of GPU-hours (a five-to-seven-figure bill for frontier models). This is precisely the cost that transfer learning (§5) is designed to avoid — and why almost nobody trains from scratch.

subsections.title: Overfitting in Deep Learning (and the Practical Fixes)

blocks.html:
Overfitting — the model memorising the training data instead of learning the general pattern — was a risk in classical ML; in deep learning it's acute, because networks have so many weights they can memorise noise outright. The eval discipline from Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation (a held-out test set, judged once) is how you detect it: training loss keeps dropping while validation loss starts rising — the model is getting better at the exam it's seen and worse at the real world.

The practical fixes are intuition-level, and you should recognise them in a plan:

FixWhat it doesPlain intuition
More dataThe single best cureHarder to memorise a million examples than a thousand; more signal, less noise-fitting
Data augmentationCheaply manufacture "new" dataFlip/rotate/crop images, paraphrase text — same label, more variety, so the model can't memorise exact inputs
DropoutRandomly switch off neurons during trainingForces the network not to over-rely on any one path — like cross-training a team so no single person is a point of failure
Early stoppingStop when validation loss stops improvingDon't keep training into the memorisation zone — quit while you're ahead
Transfer learningStart from a model that already generalises (§5)The biggest practical fix of all — fewer weights to learn from your data means far less to overfit

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Overfitting is a student who memorised last year's exam answers and is lost when the questions change. Every fix above is a way of forcing genuine understanding instead of memorisation — more questions to study (data, augmentation), studying without crutches (dropout), stopping before rote learning sets in (early stopping), or starting from someone who already understands the subject (transfer learning).

subsections.title: Transfer Learning & Fine-Tuning — the Most Important Lesson

blocks.html:
If you remember one thing from this module, remember this: you do not train deep models from scratch. You start from a pretrained model and adapt it. This is transfer learning, and it is the single move that turns deep learning from a research-budget endeavour into something a small team can deliver in weeks. It deserves — and gets here — the most space.

The idea. Someone (a big lab, an open-source community) has already spent enormous compute training a model on a giant general dataset — millions of images, or a huge text corpus. That model has already learned the hard, general part: what edges, textures, shapes, words, and grammar look like. Those learned representations (§2) are not specific to their task — they transfer. You take that pretrained model and adapt it to your narrow task with your (much smaller) data.

Two flavours, by how much you adapt:

ApproachWhat you doWhenData & compute needed
Feature extraction (freeze)Keep the pretrained model fixed; train only a small new "head" on top for your taskYour task is close to the original; you have little dataSmallest — often hundreds of examples, minutes on a modest GPU
Fine-tuningContinue training some or all of the pretrained model's weights on your data, gentlyYour task differs more; you have moderate data and want max accuracyLarger but still a fraction of from-scratch — thousands of examples, a GPU for hours not weeks

Why this collapses cost and data needs — the part that reshapes your estimates:

  • Data: from-scratch deep learning needs hundreds of thousands to millions of labelled examples. Transfer learning can get a strong result from hundreds to a few thousand — because the model already learned the general features and only needs to learn your specifics. For most clients, this is the difference between "impossible, we don't have the data" and "feasible."
  • Compute: you skip the expensive part. The thousands of GPU-hours went into the pretraining, which someone else paid for. Your adaptation is hours, not weeks — turning a five-figure compute bill into a small one.
  • Time & talent: weeks instead of months, and you need someone who can adapt and evaluate a model rather than a researcher who can design one from nothing.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Hiring a model is like hiring a person. Training from scratch is raising a child from birth to do one job — absurdly expensive and slow. Transfer learning is hiring an experienced graduate and giving them a week of onboarding for your specific role. They already know how to read, reason, and see; you only teach them your particulars. Almost every successful deep-learning mission in 2026 is "onboarding", not "raising from birth" — and your cost estimate should assume the former unless someone proves a genuine need for the latter.

blocks.title: a defect classifier from 800 photos
blocks.html:
A manufacturer wants to flag scratched parts on the line from camera images. They have ~800 labelled photos (400 good, 400 scratched) — nowhere near enough to train a vision model from scratch.

  1. From-scratch? A vision network trained from zero on 800 images would overfit instantly and fail. Off the table.
  2. Transfer learning instead. Start from a vision model pretrained on millions of general images (it already "knows" edges, textures, surfaces). Replace its final classification layer with a fresh good/scratched head.
  3. Feature extraction first. Freeze the pretrained body, train only the new head on the 800 photos — minutes on one GPU. This alone often reaches strong accuracy because "scratch vs smooth surface" leans on exactly the texture features the model already learned.
  4. Fine-tune if needed. If accuracy isn't enough, gently unfreeze the top layers and continue training at a low learning rate to specialise to this factory's lighting and parts — a few GPU-hours.
  5. Augment to fight overfitting. Rotate, flip, and adjust brightness on the 800 photos to manufacture variety (§4), so the model generalises beyond the exact shots taken.
  6. Evaluate honestly. Hold out a test set judged once; pick the threshold from the business cost of a missed defect vs a false alarm — straight from Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation. Deliverable: a defect-scoring service wired into the line's existing camera feed, not "a neural network."

What carried it: a pretrained model, 800 photos, augmentation, and a sensible threshold — days of GPU time, not a research project. That is the shape of a sellable deep-learning mission.

A concept you'll meet constantly from here on. As a deep model processes an input, an intermediate layer holds a vector of numbers that captures the input's meaning — its learned representation. That vector is an embedding. Similar inputs land at nearby points; dissimilar ones land far apart. An embedding turns "fuzzy" things (a sentence, an image, a product, a user) into coordinates you can compute with — measure similarity, cluster, search, feed into a simple classifier.

This is the quiet workhorse behind semantic search, recommendations, deduplication, and retrieval for LLMs (RAG, in RAG & Knowledge Systems). You often don't need to train anything — you call a pretrained model to turn your data into embeddings and build on top. The full treatment, including how embeddings power search and NLP, is in NLP & Embeddings in Practice; for now, just hold the picture: deep models hand you a numeric fingerprint of meaning, and that fingerprint is reusable infrastructure.
Deep learning sits in a heavier weight class than classical ML on every input, and that must feed your build-vs-buy stance and your estimates:

DimensionClassical ML (Classical ML & Forecasting in Practice)Deep learning
DataThousands of rows can sufficeLots — unless you use transfer learning (then far less)
ComputeCPU, minutes, ~€0GPUs, billed by the hour; training is the cost centre
TalentCommon, hireable data scientistsScarcer, pricier DL specialists who know the frameworks & pitfalls
Iteration speedFast — retrain in minutesSlower — experiments take hours/days
ExplainabilityDecent (SHAP, coefficients)Hard — mostly a black box (see Responsible AI: Interpretability, Fairness, Safety & the EU AI Act)

The build-vs-buy consequence: for perception and language tasks, buy the pretrained model (it's a commodity) and build only the adaptation and integration — the differentiator. From-scratch deep learning is justified only by a genuinely novel problem with no suitable pretrained model and the data and budget to match — rare, and almost never the right first move (the same escalation discipline as everywhere in this path).

Deep-learning missions sell on perception and language capability the client can't get from classical ML — but the credible, fixed-scope versions all rest on transfer learning, not from-scratch training. Sell the adaptation and integration; rent the pretrained model.

  • Transfer-Learning Classifier (Images or Text), PoC → Production — Problem: "we need to automatically classify these images/documents and we don't have a research team or a million labels." Deliverable: a trained-by-fine-tuning classifier (e.g. defect detection, document/email triage, product categorisation) delivered as a scored API or batch job wired into the client's workflow, with an honest evaluation and a chosen decision threshold. Scope: a 2-4 week PoC on a few hundred–few thousand labels to prove feasibility, then 6-12 weeks to production-harden. Who buys: a Head of Operations/Quality (vision on a line), a COO or shared-services lead (document triage), an e-commerce/catalogue owner (categorisation).

  • Deep-Learning Feasibility Study / Go–No-Go — Problem: a stakeholder wants a deep-learning capability and needs an honest, low-cost read on whether it's viable before committing real budget. Deliverable: a feasibility report — data sufficiency assessment, a transfer-learning baseline built on the client's actual data, a measured accuracy-vs-target verdict, a compute/cost projection, and a go / scope-down / no-go recommendation. Scope: 2-3 weeks, fixed price. Who buys: a CTO/CDO de-risking a bet, an innovation lead, a product owner. (High-trust, high-margin — you sell judgement and a real baseline, not a finished build; mirrors the audit/eval-plan missions in Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation.)

  • Augment an Existing Product with a Pretrained Capability — Problem: a working product needs one perception/language feature (visual search, auto-tagging, image moderation, smart OCR, voice transcription) that classical ML can't deliver. Deliverable: the feature, integrated — built on a pretrained or hosted model, exposed where the product already lives, with latency and cost within agreed bounds. Scope: 4-10 weeks depending on how much fine-tuning vs a straight API call it needs. Who buys: a Head of Product, a platform engineering lead.

Buy-vs-build: the pretrained model is the commodity — rent or download it; your billable value is the data work, the fine-tuning, the honest evaluation, and the integration. Never sell "a neural network." What to charge for: the feasibility study as fixed-price repeatable IP; the PoC and the production build as scoped phases; and — because models drift and data shifts — a monitoring/retraining retainer (real recurring revenue, see MLOps & Productionization). What NOT to promise: a specific accuracy number before you've tested transfer learning on the client's real data; from-scratch training "because it's more bespoke" (it's usually just more expensive); human-level reliability with no human-in-the-loop; or that a black-box model will be fully explainable to a regulator (flag that constraint early — Responsible AI: Interpretability, Fairness, Safety & the EU AI Act).
As of 2026 — treat names/prices as perishable; re-check before quoting.

Tool / CategoryUse hereOSS / PaidNote
PyTorchThe default deep-learning frameworkOSSWhat most teams and tutorials use; deeper code → Deep Learning Foundations (MLE Path)
TensorFlow / KerasAlternative framework, friendly high-level APIOSSStill common in enterprise; Keras is the gentle on-ramp
Hugging Face (Hub + Transformers)Pretrained models for transfer learning & embeddingsOSS + paid tiersThe single most useful place for "don't train from scratch" — vast model zoo
fast.aiHigh-level transfer-learning library & courseOSSFastest path to a working fine-tuned model for a small team
Cloud GPUs (AWS, GCP, Azure, plus GPU specialists)Training & inference computePaid (hourly)The cost centre; compare per-hour rates & spot pricing; watch egress & lock-in
Managed ML platforms (SageMaker, Vertex AI, Azure ML)One-stop train/tune/serve/monitorPaidConvenient; price and lock-in caveats — see MLOps & Productionization
Pretrained embedding models / APIsTurn text/images into vectors without trainingOSS + paidFoundation for search, similarity, RAG — detail in NLP & Embeddings in Practice
Experiment tracking (MLflow, Weights & Biases)Track runs, compare fine-tuning experimentsOSS / FreemiumDeep-learning iteration is slow — tracking pays off
The leader's read: the model is increasingly free or rented; the recurring bill is GPU time and the specialists who run it. Budget those, not the algorithm.

SituationDeep learning is overkill — do this instead
The data is tabularGradient-boosted trees — cheaper, faster, usually better, no GPU (Classical ML & Forecasting in Practice)
A few hundred examples and no pretrained model fitsClassical ML or a rule; from-scratch DL will overfit and fail
A €X/month API already does it (OCR, transcription, generic vision/chat)Buy and integrate; don't build or fine-tune anything
The logic is known and explainableWrite the rules; deep learning would only obscure a clear decision
You need a fully explainable output (regulated credit, clinical)Favour a transparent model; a black box is a liability (Responsible AI: Interpretability, Fairness, Safety & the EU AI Act)
Stakeholder wants it "because it's cutting-edge"Route by the data, not the buzzword; name the cheaper thing that clears the bar
You'd spend more on GPUs than the problem is worthRe-read the value/complexity budget; the espresso machine isn't free

The senior move, unchanged across this path: prove the cheaper thing fails before reaching for the GPU. And when deep learning is right, prove transfer learning is insufficient before training from scratch.

• Aurélien Géron — Hands-On Machine Learning (the deep-learning half) — intuition-first, the practical reference for everything here including transfer learning. (Paid)
  • François Chollet — Deep Learning with Python — exceptionally clear conceptual intuition, light on maths; the transfer-learning chapters are gold. (Paid)
  • Andrew Ng — Machine Learning Yearning (free) — not about algorithms but about strategy: data, error analysis, what to do when a model underperforms. Leader-relevant.

• fast.ai — Practical Deep Learning for Coders — the canonical "transfer learning first" course; the fastest way to internalise §5.
  • Hugging Face course & model hub docs — how pretrained models and fine-tuning work in practice.
  • 3Blue1Brown — Neural Networks video series — the best visual intuition for §2–§3 without the maths.

• Deeper math (the derivations deliberately skipped here — backprop, optimisation, initialisation, the universal approximation theorem) → Neural Network Foundations (AI Scientist Path).
  • Deeper code (building, fine-tuning, and serving deep models in PyTorch, production-grade) → Deep Learning Foundations (ML Engineer Path).

subsections.title: Decision Checklist — "Should this be a deep-learning mission?"

items.html: Is the input unstructured (images, audio, raw text)? If it's tabular, default to boosted trees instead.
Is there a pretrained model we can adapt, so we're doing transfer learning, not training from scratch?
Do we have enough labelled data for fine-tuning (hundreds–thousands), even if not the millions from-scratch would need?
Have we budgeted GPU/compute cost — and confirmed it's proportionate to the problem's value?
Can we staff the DL skills (in-house or partner), and tolerate slower iteration than classical ML?
Is the black-box nature acceptable, or is explainability a hard requirement here?
Is the deliverable wired into the client's workflow, with an honest eval and a business-set threshold?

blocks.html:
• "Let's use deep learning" for tabular data. (Boosted trees, almost always — cheaper and usually better.)
  • A plan to train from scratch when a pretrained model exists. (Burning budget on the part someone already paid for.)
  • Promising a deep model on a few hundred examples with no transfer learning. (It will overfit and fail.)
  • No GPU/compute line in the budget. (The cost is real and it's the differentiator from classical ML.)
  • "We'll just add more epochs" to fix a quality problem. (That's how you overfit, not improve.)
  • A specific accuracy promised before transfer learning was tested on the client's real data.
  • Selling deep learning where a €X/month API already does the job.
  • Ignoring that the output is a black box when the use case (credit, clinical, hiring) demands an explanation.

subsections.title: Scoping Prompts (ask the client / the team)

blocks.html:
• "Is the input something a human perceives (an image, a recording, free text) or something they tabulate?"
  • "How many labelled examples do we have today, and is there a pretrained model we can start from?"
  • "Who pays the GPU bill, and is that cost proportionate to what the prediction is worth?"
  • "Could a hosted API or an off-the-shelf model deliver this without us training anything?"
  • "Does anyone need to understand why the model decided what it did — a regulator, a customer, an auditor?"
  • "Where will the prediction appear so someone actually acts on it — and what happens when it's wrong?"
Before moving on, you should be able to:

items.html: Route a problem to deep learning vs classical ML from its data type and scale, and justify it in one sentence
Explain a neural network as stacked transformations learning representations, with no equations
Describe loss, gradient descent, and backprop-as-blame-assignment intuitively, and name what epochs / batch size / learning rate trade off
Explain why transfer learning collapses the data, compute, and talent cost of a deep-learning mission — and default to it
Estimate, at a rough order of magnitude, the GPU/compute cost of a deep-learning effort and put it in a quote
Explain what an embedding is and why it's reusable infrastructure (forward to NLP & Embeddings in Practice)
Scope a transfer-learning classifier, a feasibility study, or a "add a pretrained capability" mission with a named deliverable, realistic phases, and the buyer
